steps.title: Locate the first Round Table source
steps.content: The passage gives a timeline for Arthurian sources. Geoffrey of Monmouth's History of the Kings of Britain was written in the 1130s, and Wace later adapted some of that material into Roman de Brut in 1155. The key contrast is that Roman de Brut mentions the Round Table, while nothing written before 1155 does.

steps.title: Reject unsupported comparisons
steps.content: A, "Geoffrey of Monmouth's accounts of Arthurian legends in his History are more similar overall in content to the accounts in Tom a Lincoln than they are to the accounts in Roman de Brut," makes an overall comparison the passage never provides. B, "Geoffrey of Monmouth was unaware of stories of the Round Table when composing his History, though historians know that works containing such stories were available to him," adds a claim about what was available to Geoffrey that is not stated.

steps.title: Choose the lineage inference
steps.content: D, "Roman de Brut is more historically accurate than History, because Tom a Lincoln had not been written when Geoffrey of Monmouth was writing his History," introduces historical accuracy, which the passage never evaluates. C, "any Arthurian legend that mentions the Round Table likely has a lineage that connects it to Roman de Brut," follows from the fact that Roman de Brut is the first written source named in the passage to include the Round Table. The answer is C.
